insights: Breadth: 1/16 stocks up — broadly weak today
context: Broad selling today — most large-caps fell. Down days are exactly when disciplined SIP investors keep buying units cheaper.
Money rotated into FMCG and out of Realty. Sector leadership shifts often — chasing last month's hottest sector is a classic beginner trap.
India VIX is low (13.29) — calm, low expected volatility. Calm markets breed complacency; a good time to check your asset allocation, not to chase risk.

gk.t: Why there's an International Date Line
gk.teaser: Cross it westward and you skip a day.
gk.more: The Earth has 24 time zones; somewhere the calendar must flip. The line zigzags through the Pacific to avoid splitting countries. Fly Delhi to San Francisco and you can land at an earlier local time than you took off — the date line gives the day back.

gk.teaser: It replaced a maze of central and state levies.
gk.more: The Goods and Services Tax merged excise, VAT, service tax and more into one destination-based tax with slabs (0/5/12/18/28%). It made interstate commerce dramatically simpler — trucks no longer queue at state borders for tax checks — and every invoice feeds a single national IT backbone.

gk.teaser: In 2023 India became the first to land near the lunar south pole.
gk.more: After Chandrayaan-2's lander crashed in 2019, ISRO's Vikram lander touched down softly on 23 August 2023 — making India the fourth country to soft-land on the Moon and the first at the south polar region, where ice deposits could support future missions. Mission cost: roughly ₹615 crore, less than many Hollywood films.

gk.teaser: The first message in 1969 was meant to be 'LOGIN' — it crashed at 'LO'.
gk.more: A US defence research network connecting four universities grew into the internet after TCP/IP became its common language in 1983. The web (websites, URLs, browsers) came later — invented by Tim Berners-Lee at CERN in 1989 as a layer on top of the internet.

gk.teaser: Chip transistor counts doubled roughly every two years for 50 years.
gk.more: Intel co-founder Gordon Moore's 1965 observation became the tech industry's metronome — it's why phones outpower old supercomputers. Physical limits have slowed it, which is partly why the industry pivoted to specialised chips (GPUs, AI accelerators) instead of just faster general processors.

gk.teaser: The chip element, not the software.
gk.more: The Santa Clara Valley earned the name in the 1970s from silicon semiconductor makers like Fairchild and Intel. Software, internet and AI giants came in later waves. Bengaluru earned the 'India's Silicon Valley' tag through the 1990s IT services boom — Infosys and Wipro, not chips.

gk.teaser: A harmless preview teaches your immune system the enemy's face.
gk.more: Vaccines present a dead, weakened, or fragment version of a pathogen (or, in mRNA vaccines, instructions to build one protein of it), so your body develops antibodies without the disease. Smallpox — which killed ~300 million people in the 20th century alone — became the first disease ever eradicated, in 1980.

gk.teaser: India is still crashing into Asia at ~5 cm a year.
gk.more: Around 50 million years ago the Indian plate slammed into the Eurasian plate; the crumple zone is the Himalayas, still rising a few millimetres yearly. The same collision makes the region earthquake-prone — the reason Delhi sits in seismic zone IV.

gk.teaser: Air scatters blue light more than red.
gk.more: Sunlight contains all colours; molecules in the atmosphere scatter shorter (bluer) wavelengths far more — called Rayleigh scattering. At sunset, light travels through more air, the blue scatters away before reaching you, leaving reds and oranges.

gk.teaser: Gravity so strong that light can't leave.
gk.more: When a massive star collapses, its gravity can warp space so severely nothing escapes past the 'event horizon'. They're detected by their effect on nearby matter. The first-ever photo of one (M87*, 2019) took a telescope network the size of Earth.

gkMore.t: The Great Wall of China isn't visible from space
gkMore.teaser: The naked-eye claim is a stubborn myth.
gkMore.more: Contrary to popular belief, the Great Wall cannot be seen from space with the unaided eye — it is very long but only a few metres wide, blending into the surrounding terrain. Astronauts have confirmed they cannot pick it out from orbit without help. City lights and airport runways are far easier to spot from the International Space Station.

gkMore.t: The Sahara is nearly the size of the United States
gkMore.more: The Sahara covers about 9 million square kilometres, roughly the area of the United States or China. Yet it is not the biggest desert overall — Antarctica, a frozen desert, is larger. The Sahara was green with lakes and grassland just a few thousand years ago, and will likely turn green again as Earth's orbit slowly shifts.

gkMore.t: Sunlight takes about eight minutes to reach Earth
gkMore.teaser: You always see the Sun as it was, never as it is.
gkMore.more: Light travels at about 300,000 kilometres per second, but the Sun is so distant that its light takes roughly 8 minutes and 20 seconds to arrive. If the Sun vanished, we would keep seeing and feeling it for those eight minutes. Light from the next-nearest star takes over four years to reach us.

gkMore.t: You are literally made of stardust
gkMore.teaser: The atoms in your body were forged inside stars.
gkMore.more: Almost every element heavier than hydrogen — the carbon in your cells, the oxygen you breathe, the iron in your blood — was created inside stars and flung out when they died. Those atoms drifted through space, gathered to form our Sun and planets, and eventually became you. As astronomer Carl Sagan put it, we are 'star stuff.'

gkMore.t: Tiny 'water bears' can survive the vacuum of space
gkMore.more: Tardigrades, or water bears, are half-millimetre creatures that can endure being boiled, frozen near absolute zero, dried out for years, and even exposed to the vacuum and radiation of open space. They manage it by curling into a shrivelled, near-dead state called cryptobiosis. Scientists have revived specimens after decades in deep freeze.

gkMore.t: Most global trade is still priced in US dollars
gkMore.teaser: A legacy of a deal struck in 1944.
gkMore.more: After World War II, the 1944 Bretton Woods agreement pegged major currencies to the US dollar, which was itself convertible to gold. Even after that gold link ended in 1971, the dollar remained the world's dominant reserve and trade currency, especially for oil — the so-called petrodollar. This gives the US enormous financial leverage, which rivals increasingly try to reduce.

gkMore.t: The Rule of 72 tells you when your money doubles
gkMore.teaser: Just divide 72 by the interest rate.
gkMore.more: Compound interest means you earn returns on your past returns, so money snowballs over time. A handy shortcut, the Rule of 72, estimates how long an investment takes to double: divide 72 by the annual return. At 8% a year, money doubles in about nine years — which is why starting early beats investing large sums late.

gkMore.t: The first webcam was invented to watch a coffee pot
gkMore.teaser: Cambridge scientists were tired of empty pots.
gkMore.more: In 1991, researchers at Cambridge University pointed a camera at a coffee pot so they could check from their desks whether it was full before walking over. Connected to the web in 1993, it became the world's first webcam. The famous 'Trojan Room coffee pot' was switched off in 2001 and auctioned online.

gkMore.t: The QWERTY keyboard was built to prevent jams
gkMore.teaser: It deliberately separates the letters you type together.
gkMore.more: The QWERTY layout was designed in the 1870s for mechanical typewriters, spacing common letter pairs far apart so the metal typebars were less likely to clash and jam. When typewriters gave way to computers, the mechanical reason disappeared, but the layout was far too entrenched to replace. Faster designs like Dvorak exist, but never caught on.

gkMore.t: The Kumbh Mela is the largest human gathering on Earth
gkMore.teaser: Tens of millions converge at India's sacred rivers.
gkMore.more: The Hindu Kumbh Mela, held on a rotating cycle at sites like Prayagraj, draws the biggest crowds of people anywhere on the planet. On peak days tens of millions bathe at the river confluence, and the temporary tent city grows so vast it shows up clearly in satellite images. UNESCO recognises it as intangible cultural heritage of humanity.

learn.title: What happens when you load a web page
learn.teaser: The elegant plumbing beneath every click.
body: When you type an address and hit enter, your request does not travel as one continuous stream. Instead, the data is broken into small chunks called packets, each individually addressed and sent off across the network. Guided by a family of rules known as TCP/IP, these packets hop from router to router — often taking different paths — and are reassembled in the correct order at the destination. There is no central controller directing traffic; the system is deliberately decentralised.

Two pieces of infrastructure make this feel instant. The Domain Name System (DNS) acts like the internet's phone book, translating a human-friendly name like a website's address into the numerical IP address computers actually use to find each other. And most intercontinental traffic does not go by satellite but through a web of fibre-optic cables lying on the ocean floor, carrying data between continents at nearly the speed of light.

This architecture is a direct inheritance from the internet's origins in ARPANET, which was designed so that data could reroute around damaged nodes and keep flowing. That resilience is why the network scales so gracefully and rarely goes fully dark. Every web page you open is the product of thousands of these tiny packets racing across the planet and back, usually in a fraction of a second.

learn.title: The AI shift: what large language models actually do
learn.teaser: Powerful pattern machines, not digital minds.
body: The recent wave of artificial intelligence is driven by large language models (LLMs) — the technology behind modern AI chatbots. At their core, these systems are trained on vast quantities of text and learn to do something deceptively simple: predict the next word in a sequence. By doing this billions of times over enormous datasets, they absorb the statistical patterns of language, along with a great deal of the knowledge encoded in that text, and can then generate fluent, often strikingly useful responses.

It helps to be clear about what this is and isn't. An LLM does not 'understand' the world the way a person does, nor does it look up facts in a database. It generates plausible continuations based on patterns, which is why it can be articulate and helpful but also confidently wrong — producing 'hallucinations,' or inheriting biases present in its training data. The breakthrough that made today's models possible was a neural-network design called the transformer, introduced in 2017, combined with vast computing power.

The economic and social implications are still unfolding. AI is already reshaping how people write, code, research and create, and it poses particular questions for economies like India's, with its large services and IT sector. The realistic near-term picture is less science-fiction robot and more a powerful general-purpose tool — one that amplifies human work, demands new skills, and requires care about accuracy, fairness and appropriate use.

learn.title: Why tiny chips decide global power
learn.teaser: The most important industry most people never think about.
body: Semiconductors — the microchips inside phones, cars, appliances, weapons and data centres — have become the foundation of the modern economy. What makes them geopolitically explosive is how extraordinarily concentrated their production is. The most advanced chips are manufactured by a handful of firms, above all Taiwan's TSMC, and the ultra-precise machines needed to etch them are made by essentially one company, ASML in the Netherlands, while much of the cutting-edge design comes from the United States.

This means no single country can make a leading-edge chip alone; the supply chain is a globally intertwined web with critical choke points. That fragility became painfully visible during the shortages of 2021, when a shortfall of chips idled car factories worldwide. It has also turned semiconductors into a central front in the rivalry between the United States and China, with export controls on advanced chips and chipmaking equipment used as tools of statecraft.

Taiwan's dominance in particular gives the island outsized strategic importance and adds a dangerous edge to tensions over its status. Governments have responded by pouring money into building domestic chip factories, or 'fabs,' to reduce their dependence — including India, which is courting fabrication and assembly plants as part of its push to become a manufacturing hub. In the coming decades, who makes the world's chips may matter as much as who controls its oil once did.

learn.title: P/E ratio: the market's price tag
learn.teaser: How many years of profit you're paying for one share.
body: Price-to-Earnings = share price ÷ earnings per share. A P/E of 25 means you pay ₹25 for every ₹1 of annual profit. High P/E signals the market expects fast growth (or the stock is expensive); low P/E signals slow growth expectations (or a bargain).

It's only meaningful against context: the company's own history, its sector average, and growth rate. Indian IT often trades at 20–30; PSU banks far lower. A 'cheap' stock with shrinking profits is usually cheap for a reason.

learn.title: Correlation is not causation
learn.teaser: The most important sentence in analytics.
body: Ice cream sales and drownings rise together — summer causes both. When two metrics move together, there are always four possibilities: A causes B, B causes A, something else causes both, or coincidence. Dashboards show correlation; only controlled experiments or careful causal analysis show causation.
Practical BI reflex: before reporting 'X drove Y', ask what else changed in the same window — a campaign, a price change, seasonality, a tracking change. The last one bites analysts most often.

learn.title: Mean vs median: which lies less?
learn.teaser: One billionaire in a room makes the 'average' person rich.
body: The mean (sum ÷ count) is dragged by outliers; the median (middle value) isn't. Average income sounds impressive when a few earn crores; median income tells you what the typical person makes. Same for ticket sizes, session times, order values.

Rule of thumb: for skewed data (money, wait times, engagement), report the median or percentiles. If mean and median differ a lot, that gap itself is a finding — your distribution has a long tail worth investigating.

learn.title: Opportunity cost
learn.teaser: The real cost of anything is what you gave up to get it.
body: Spending ₹15 lakh on a car isn't just ₹15 lakh — it's also the ~₹47 lakh that money could become in 10 years at 12%. Every choice of time or money silently declines the alternatives. Economists call the best foregone alternative the opportunity cost.

It reframes decisions: a 'free' three-hour meeting costs the work you'd have shipped. An okay job offer costs the better one you stopped searching for. Comparing against 'doing nothing' is almost always the wrong baseline.

learn.title: How IPOs actually work
learn.teaser: A private company sells shares to the public for the first time.
body: The company hires investment banks to set a price band, files a prospectus (DRHP) with SEBI, and collects bids. Retail investors apply in lots; oversubscribed issues allot by lottery. On listing day the stock starts trading — sometimes far above or below issue price.

Listing 'pops' make headlines, but the base rate is sobering: many hyped IPOs trade below issue price within a year (India's 2021 tech-IPO batch is the classic case). Reading where the money goes — fresh growth capital vs early investors cashing out — tells you a lot.
